Job sits in his ashes and remembers. Not the present—the crushing weight of boils, the stench, the relentless questioning of his friends. He remembers when God's lamp shone above his head, when the Almighty rested upon his tent, when his children surrounded him and streams of oil poured from the rock. This is not the nostalgia of a man grasping at pleasant memories to escape his pain. This is the testimony of a man who *knows* what the presence of God feels like, and now knows the absence. The intensity of his longing cuts both ways: it proves he once experienced something real, something undeniable. He was not deceived about God's goodness. The light was real. The friendship was real. And its loss is therefore unbearable.

What makes Job's recollection so piercing is that he connects God's presence directly to his righteousness and influence. When God watched over him, he could rescue the poor, comfort the dying, restore the widow's joy. He was eyes to the blind, feet to the lame, a father to the needy. His words fell like dew and rain on eager hearts. Princes fell silent in his presence. Young men withdrew when they saw him. This was not hollow vanity—Job is describing a man whose life *mattered*, whose counsel brought healing, whose justice was woven into the fabric of his community. And he believed it would last. "I will die in my nest," he says. "My glory is ever new within me." He expected to grow old in righteousness, his roots spreading deeper, his strength renewed.

The brutal heart of this chapter is that Job is describing not merely personal loss, but betrayal by the apparent logic of God's world. A man who lived justly, who used his strength to protect the weak, who spoke truth that settled like dew on broken hearts—this man should have continued. This is how the world *ought* to work. If righteousness and God's presence produce such good, then surely they continue. Surely they secure a man's future. Yet Job now sits covered in sores, and everyone has abandoned him. The princes do not withdraw; they mock. The young men do not fear him; they laugh. His words do not fall like dew; they fall on ears that will not hear. He is trying to make sense of a universe where the rules he lived by have been catastrophically overturned. And the deepest wound is not the loss of comfort or status, but the loss of certainty about who God is and what His presence means.
